title: Watery diarrhea with episodic flushing

chart.oneLiner: 58F presents with several months of watery, non-bloody diarrhea and episodic facial flushing, with intermittent wheezing and palpitations.

opening: For months now I keep getting these sudden bouts of watery diarrhea, and my face goes red and hot out of nowhere.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old woman, mildly flushed across the face and upper chest, otherwise comfortable. Good historian, a little anxious about what is causing the spells.

HPI: 4–6 months of recurrent WATERY, secretory, NON-bloody diarrhea (often 5–10 loose stools per day, sometimes waking her at night). Separate from the stools she gets sudden FLUSHING of the face, neck, and upper chest lasting a few minutes, with a warm, sometimes tingly feeling, occasional palpitations and lightheadedness. Spells can be triggered by red wine, aged cheese, large meals, and stress/exertion (only if asked about triggers). At times she gets WHEEZING/chest tightness with the flushing spells. ~8 lb unintentional weight loss. Vague intermittent right-sided abdominal fullness/cramping. NO bloody stools, NO recent antibiotics, NO travel, NO new medications, NO clear infectious contacts. NO heat intolerance, NO neck swelling, NO tremor (pertinent negatives against thyroid disease — only if asked).
PMH: hypertension, mild. Was told years ago of a 'spot on the liver' on an ultrasound but never followed up (only if asked about prior imaging/liver). MEDS: amlodipine. No PPIs, no laxatives, no recent antibiotics. SOCIAL: occasional red wine (which seems to set off the spells); nonsmoker; lives with husband. FH: no known endocrine tumors or MEN syndromes (if asked).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: intermittent flushing of the face, neck, and upper chest; a few fine telangiectasias over the cheeks.
- Vitals: BP 104/64, HR 96, RR 18, 37.0°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Cardiac: a soft holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (tricuspid regurgitation, carcinoid heart disease) if auscultated; JVP mildly elevated if assessed.
- Pulmonary: occasional expiratory wheeze if examined during/after a spell.
- Abdomen: mild RUQ fullness with a firm, mildly enlarged liver edge if examined; non-tender, no rebound.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the dietary/alcohol triggers, the prior 'liver spot,' family history, or the relationship between flushing and the diarrhea unless asked.
- Hepatomegaly, the murmur, elevated JVP, and wheeze are revealed only if those areas are specifically examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Urine5HIAA: 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA (serotonin metabolite) MARKEDLY ELEVATED — clinches a serotonin-secreting neuroendocrine tumor.
labs.ChromograninA: Plasma chromogranin A elevated (neuroendocrine tumor marker; supportive, less specific).
labs.Imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis: ileal/mesenteric mass with multiple hypervascular LIVER METASTASES — the metastatic burden allowing serotonin to bypass hepatic first-pass and cause systemic syndrome.
labs.SSTRimaging: 68Ga-DOTATATE PET (somatostatin-receptor imaging) shows avid uptake in the primary and hepatic lesions — confirms a well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor.
labs.Echo: Echocardiogram: thickened, retracted tricuspid leaflets with tricuspid regurgitation and right-heart involvement (carcinoid heart disease).
labs.Workup: Stool studies (cultures, C. difficile toxin, ova/parasites) NEGATIVE and TSH NORMAL — excludes infection and thyroid disease.

ekg.findings: Sinus rhythm with tall, peaked P waves in the inferior leads (P pulmonale) suggesting right atrial enlargement; no acute ischemia.

cxr.verify: Source a study matching: clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence / no acute process. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lungs and subtle right heart prominence
searchTerms: normal chest x-ray right heart border
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 63-year-old woman with recurrent flushing, watery diarrhea, and exertional breathlessness undergoes chest imaging during her evaluation.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this chronic diarrhea with flushing (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Carcinoid syndrome / neuroendocrine tumor (leading,7). Must EXCLUDE infection incl. C. difficile and chronic infectious/secretory diarrhea (CANT-MISS,5). Other secretory/endocrine tumors — VIPoma, gastrinoma, medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma (core,3). Thyrotoxicosis (core,2). Idiopathic flushing / mastocytosis / menopausal vasomotor symptoms (core,2). Names a secretory diarrhea with flushing and the imperative to measure urinary 5-HIAA while excluding infection/thyroid disease (core,6).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: infectious/secretory diarrhea including C. difficile; other secretory endocrine tumors (VIPoma, gastrinoma, medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma). Also reasonable: thyrotoxicosis; idiopathic flushing / mastocytosis / menopausal flushing.

criticalActions.item: Exclude infectious/secretory diarrhea incl. C. difficile

coreActions.item: Other secretory/endocrine tumors (VIPoma, gastrinoma, MTC, pheochromocytoma)
Idiopathic flushing / mastocytosis / menopausal vasomotor symptoms
Names a secretory diarrhea with flushing and the imperative to measure urinary 5-HIAA while excluding infection/thyroid disease

mustNotMiss: Exclude infectious/secretory diarrhea incl. C. difficile
Other secretory/endocrine tumors (VIPoma, gastrinoma, MTC, pheochromocytoma)
acceptableAlternatives: Idiopathic flushing / mastocytosis / menopausal vasomotor symptoms

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA — serotonin metabolite (CANT-MISS,4). Plasma chromogranin A (2). Stool studies + TSH to EXCLUDE infection and thyroid disease (CANT-MISS,3). Cross-sectional imaging — CT/MRI abdomen for primary and liver metastases (3). Somatostatin-receptor imaging (68Ga-DOTATATE PET) to localize/stage (2). Echocardiogram to assess for carcinoid heart disease (1). PENALTY: provoking the tumor — e.g., unprotected stimulation/biopsy or skipping octreotide cover before procedures, risking carcinoid crisis (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Urine 5 HIAA; Chromogranin A; Imaging; SST rimaging; Echo; Workup.

criticalActions.item: Stool studies + TSH to exclude infection and thyroid disease

coreActions.item: Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI abdomen) for primary and liver metastases

penalties.item: provoking the tumor without octreotide cover, risking carcinoid crisis

requiredTests: Stool studies + TSH to exclude infection and thyroid disease
Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI abdomen) for primary and liver metastases
contraindicatedTests: provoking the tumor without octreotide cover, risking carcinoid crisis
orderSensitiveActions: provoking the tumor without octreotide cover, risking carcinoid crisis

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus rhythm with peaked P waves (P pulmonale) suggesting right atrial enlargement; no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes peaked P waves / right atrial enlargement and links it to right-heart strain (5); systematic read with pertinent negatives (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia or missing the right atrial abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: tall, peaked P waves in the inferior leads (P pulmonale)
names right atrial enlargement and links it to right-heart involvement
criticalFindings: Peaked P waves (P pulmonale) of right atrial enlargement.
commonMistakes: calling the tracing entirely normal and missing the right atrial enlargement
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus rhythm with tall, peaked P waves (P pulmonale) indicating right atrial enlargement; no acute ischemic changes — fits right-heart involvement of carcinoid heart disease.

criticalActions: Peaked P waves (P pulmonale) of right atrial enlargement.
coreActions: tall, peaked P waves in the inferior leads (P pulmonale)
names right atrial enlargement and links it to right-heart involvement

steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence; no acute pulmonary process. CREDIT: identifies clear lungs / no acute process (6); notes subtle right heart prominence and that it screens for right-heart involvement (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: clear lungs, no acute pulmonary process
notes subtle right heart border prominence as a clue to right-heart involvement
criticalFindings: Clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence; no acute process.
commonMistakes: mistaking the right heart prominence for a mass or calling a fictitious infiltrate
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lungs with no acute pulmonary process; subtle prominence of the right heart border consistent with right-heart involvement of carcinoid heart disease.

criticalActions: Clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence; no acute process.
coreActions: clear lungs, no acute pulmonary process
notes subtle right heart border prominence as a clue to right-heart involvement

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Urinary 5-HIAA is markedly elevated, chromogranin A is high, CT shows an ileal/mesenteric mass with liver metastases, DOTATATE PET is avid, and stool studies and TSH are normal. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (6). Cites elevated 5-HIAA/chromogranin A with hepatic metastases (explaining systemic serotonin escape) and DOTATATE-avid disease; explicitly excludes infection and thyroid disease via negative stool studies and normal TSH (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor. Supported by markedly elevated urinary 5-HIAA and chromogranin A with hepatic metastases (explaining systemic serotonin escape) and DOTATATE-avid disease. This excludes infection (negative stool studies) and thyroid disease (normal TSH).
mustNotMiss: Exclude infectious/secretory diarrhea incl. C. difficile
acceptableAlternatives: Metastatic neuroendocrine tumor without classic syndrome

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH neuroendocrine tumor / carcinoid: somatostatin analog (octreotide/lanreotide) first-line to control flushing and diarrhea and to provide antiproliferative effect (CANT-MISS,7); octreotide cover to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis around procedures, anesthesia, or hypotension (CANT-MISS,4); avoid known triggers — alcohol, large meals, certain amines; manage carcinoid heart disease and consider valve surgery (3); locoregional/cytoreductive options for liver metastases — resection, hepatic-artery embolization, PRRT (4); niacin supplementation for niacin/pellagra deficiency (2); oncology/endocrine surgery referral and staging (3). PENALTY: giving catecholamines/unopposed sympathomimetics or proceeding to anesthesia/biopsy without octreotide, precipitating carcinoid crisis (-3); starting empiric antimotility/steroids as if IBD without working up the secretory cause (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Carcinoid syndrome: start a somatostatin analog (octreotide or long-acting lanreotide) to control flushing/diarrhea and slow tumor growth. Provide octreotide cover before any procedure, anesthesia, or with hypotension to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis (avoid catecholamines). Counsel to avoid triggers (alcohol, large meals). Address carcinoid heart disease (echo, cardiology, consider tricuspid valve surgery). For hepatic metastases, consider resection/cytoreduction, hepatic-artery embolization, or peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT). Supplement niacin to prevent pellagra. Refer to oncology and surgery; stage and follow with chromogranin A/5-HIAA.

criticalActions.item: Octreotide cover to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis

coreActions.item: Locoregional/cytoreductive therapy for liver metastases (resection, embolization, PRRT)
Oncology / endocrine surgery referral and staging
Manage carcinoid heart disease (echo, consider valve surgery)
Avoid known triggers (alcohol, large meals, amines)

penalties.item: Catecholamines / procedure without octreotide precipitating carcinoid crisis

requiredActions: Octreotide cover to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis
optionalActions: Locoregional/cytoreductive therapy for liver metastases (resection, embolization, PRRT)
Oncology / endocrine surgery referral and staging

historyTriggers.response: I have a glass of red wine now and then — and honestly, the wine seems to set off the red, hot flushing in my face.
It's been going on for about four to six months now, and it's slowly gotten more frequent.

triggerConcepts: what do the stools look like
historyTriggers.response: It's very watery, five to ten times a day, sometimes waking me at night. There's no blood and no mucus that I've noticed.
Out of nowhere my face, neck, and upper chest go bright red and hot for a few minutes, sometimes with a tingly feeling and my heart racing.
The flushing seems to come on with red wine, aged cheese, big meals, and when I'm stressed or exerting myself.
Sometimes when I get flushed I also get a bit wheezy and my chest feels tight, but it passes.
I've lost about eight pounds without trying over these months.
Years ago an ultrasound showed a spot on my liver, but I felt fine so I never followed up on it.
No, I don't feel hot all the time, no shaking hands, and no swelling in my neck.
I only take amlodipine for blood pressure. No laxatives, no recent antibiotics, and I haven't traveled anywhere.

physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur appreciated.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Pulmonary: occasional expiratory wheeze during or after a flushing spell.
Abdomen: mild RUQ fullness with a firm, mildly enlarged liver edge (hepatic metastases); non-tender, no rebound.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Liver edge not palpable below the costal margin.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58F presents with several months of watery, non-bloody secretory diarrhea and episodic facial flushing, with intermittent wheezing and palpitations. The diagnosis is Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: Episodic flushing of the face/neck/chest with watery secretory diarrhea
Triggers of red wine, aged cheese, and large meals
Intermittent wheezing during spells and unintentional weight loss
A previously noted, never-followed-up 'spot on the liver'
keyPhysicalExamFindings: General: intermittent flushing of the face, neck, and upper chest; a few fine telangiectasias over the cheeks.
Cardiac: soft holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (tricuspid regurgitation of carcinoid heart disease).
Abdomen: mild RUQ fullness with a firm, mildly enlarged liver edge (hepatic metastases); non-tender, no rebound.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Carcinoid syndrome while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Carcinoid syndrome and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Catecholamines / procedure without octreotide precipitating carcinoid crisis
Treating empirically as IBD/infection without measuring urinary 5-HIAA
managementPearls: Octreotide cover to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis
criticalWorkupItems: Stool studies + TSH to exclude infection and thyroid disease
Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI abdomen) for primary and liver metastases
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus rhythm with peaked P waves (P pulmonale) of right atrial enlargement; no acute ischemia.
Clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence; no acute process.
criticalManagementItems: Octreotide cover to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Peaked P waves (P pulmonale) of right atrial enlargement.
Clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence; no acute process.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Peaked P waves (P pulmonale) of right atrial enlargement.
Clear lungs with subtle right heart border prominence; no acute process.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
